coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive understanding of multiple emissive states in S and N doped carbon dots and the highly selective detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Ganiga, Manjunatha
Mani, Neema Pallikkarathodi
Cyriac, Jobin
coredata.dc:description: Although exceptional progress has been observed in enhancing the quantum yield and tuning the emission of carbon dots (CDs) in the recent past, the origin of photoluminescence is hazy due to the structural complexity. In this work, we synthesised luminescent S and N co-doped CDs, termed as SNCDs, via hydrothermal carbonisation of glutathione. We performed detailed photophysical studies, including fluorescence excitation, emission and time resolved analysis to understand its pH dependence luminescence in the SNCDs. Based on the results, a comprehensive energy level diagram for the SNCDs has been proposed, explaining the origin of multiple emissive states in SNCDs, contributed by core, surface, and edge states. The results are useful in tuning the emission properties of CDs and related materials for various optical applications. We demonstrate a highly selective fluorescence detection strategy for the Cr(VI) using the SNCDs as a fluorophore.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH-dependent PL response and understanding the origin of PL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of SNCDs and inset shows the size distribution histogram for SNCDs extracted from the TEM images (B) AFM image of SNCDs. Height profile obtained from AFM is given as inset of (B).
High-resolution XPS spectra of (A) C 1s, (B) N 1s, (C) O 1s and (D) S 2p peaks of SNCDs.

Fluorescence spectra of SNCDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm) showing three emission bands viz., 390 nm shoulder band (band I), emission at 430 nm (band II) and an excitation dependent emission >430 nm (band III). Inset is the excitation spectrum collected at 430 nm showing three excitation bands at 245, 325 and 370 nm.
Emission spectra of SNCDs recorded at different excitation wavelengths at (A) pH 2, (B) pH 7, (C) pH 12 and (D) a plot of the intensity of 430 nm versus pH.
Excitation spectra of SNCDs recorded at 500 nm emission wavelengths, maintained at (A) pH 2, (B) pH 7, (C) pH 12 and (D) plot of the intensity of 330 nm band versus different pH.
Energy level diagram of SNCDs demonstrating the origin of luminescence. LE state: locally excited state, SR state: due to solvent-induced relaxation.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of SNCDs-DPC at different Cr(VI) concentrations. For comparison, PL spectra of pure SNCDs and the SNCDs-DPC mixture was also given. Excitation wavelength: 470 nm. (B) The plot of intensity at 500 nm versus the concentration of Cr(VI).

Time-resolved fluorescence results of SNCDs at different pH conditions. τ
1 and τ
2 are the lifetime components and A
1 and A
2 are the corresponding amplitudes, respectively. Lifetime fitting has been done using data analysis software by keeping values of χ2 < 1.3 (best fit).

head.ce:title: A comprehensive understanding of multiple emissive states in S and N doped carbon dots and the highly selective detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram, 695 547, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram, 695 547, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although exceptional progress has been observed in enhancing the quantum yield and tuning the emission of carbon dots (CDs) in the recent past, the origin of photoluminescence is hazy due to the structural complexity. In this work, we synthesised luminescent S and N co-doped CDs, termed as SNCDs, via hydrothermal carbonisation of glutathione. We performed detailed photophysical studies, including fluorescence excitation, emission and time resolved analysis to understand its pH dependence luminescence in the SNCDs. Based on the results, a comprehensive energy level diagram for the SNCDs has been proposed, explaining the origin of multiple emissive states in SNCDs, contributed by core, surface, and edge states. The results are useful in tuning the emission properties of CDs and related materials for various optical applications. We demonstrate a highly selective fluorescence detection strategy for the Cr(VI) using the SNCDs as a fluorophore.

ce:keyword.ce:text: S and N co-doped Carbon Dots

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as appealing nanomaterials in the family of luminescent materials because of their feeble cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, versatile and expeditious synthetic routes and appreciable photostability. There have been a variety of precursors; both natural sources and simple lab chemicals are used as precursors of CDs using diverse top-down and bottom-up synthetic schemes [1–3]. In general, CDs encompass an amorphous aromatic core feathered by various functional groups at the surface. Due to its easy and low-cost synthetic strategies and attractive properties, CDs have been scrutinised in a broad spectrum of applications, including bioimaging, sensing, light-harvesting and storage, etc. They can be a favourable alternative to toxic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1,4,5]. The most captivating feature of CDs is their ability to fluoresce, which is absent or feeble in the case of their parental counterparts such as fullerene, graphene and carbon nanotubes. In general, CDs possess a broad, excitation wavelength-dependent luminescence, with the most intense emission focussed on the blue part of the electromagnetic spectrum. Further, doping and surface passivation have been two excellent ways to enhance the quantum yield and to modulate the energy levels of CDs [6–9]. Although adequate success has been received in amplifying the quantum yield and tuning the luminescence output from CDs, the rationale behind its broad and excitation dependent emission output is unclear [10–13]. This is due to the complex structure of CDs in terms of size of the core and nature of surface/edge states, which differs according to the nature of synthetic strategies adopted, dispersion media, nature of doping, to name a few. Ding et al. demonstrated that the luminescence of CDs could be tuned by controlling the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface [14]. By developing a model system comprising three types of polyaromatic hydrocarbons, Fu et al. explained the possible origin of large stokes shift and excitation dependent spectral output in CDs [15]. Using the computational method, Sarkar et al. showed that graphitic nitrogen doping causes red-shifted absorption and subsequent variation in the photoluminescence (PL) output of CDs. In contrast, the presence of pyridinic, pyrrolic and amino centres do not influence its photophysical behaviour [15]. Another study claims the existence of multimission states in citric acid derived CDs. The emission at higher energy stems from core states, whereas the emission at lower energy is mainly due to surface states [16].

Besides, several studies report that CDs show distinct PL responses towards the pH of the medium. For example, pH dependent tunable luminescence response from amino functionalised CDs has been demonstrated [17]. CDs prepared by the carbonisation of amino acids manifested high acidophilic luminescence as revealed by Lu et al. [18] On the contrary, CDs synthesised by pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid were strongly fluorescing at basic pH, whereas PL collapses at the acidic pH conditions [19]. Based on the above reports and many others, one can conclude that extensive studies are necessitated to demystify the origin of PL behaviour in CDs and related materials.

Many industrial processes such as wood treatment, metallurgy, pigment manufacturing release Cr(VI) compounds into the environment. Although chromium in its +3 state is biologically significant in activating specific enzymes in mammals, the +6 state is potentially cytotoxic and carcinogenic [20]. The commonly used detection strategies for Cr(VI) such as atomic absorption/emission, inductively coupled mass spectrometry, colorimetric and electrochemical methods suffer from low sensitivity and selectivity, cumbersome sample pre-treatment steps and are time-consuming. Hence, fast and facile detection strategies that selectively recognise Cr (VI) with high sensitivity are most demanded.

In the present report, we demonstrate a facile approach for synthesising nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CDs (SNCDs) using glutathione as the sole source without using an acid or base. The luminescence of SNCDs was found to be pH dependant, which is highest in acidic medium, drops at neutral pH and then decreases linearly as the pH was increased above 7. A comprehensive study has been carried out to understand the origin of pH-dependent PL in SNCDs. Various spectroscopic studies reveal multiple emissive states in SNCDs in which higher energy emission stems from carbon core. In contrast, medium energy emission originates from the contribution of both edge and surface states. Further, we demonstrated a ‘turn-off’ fluorescence detection of Cr(VI), which operates via inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms, confirmed by steady state and time-resolved fluorescence spectral analysis. Here, the introduction of Cr(VI) to the solution containing SNCDs and diphenylcarbazide (DPC) quenches the emission of SNCDs. The strong absorption of DPC-Cr(VI) complex at fluorophore's excitation wavelength and good spectral overlap between the emission of donor and absorption of the acceptor led to the efficient energy transfer from the fluorophore to the quencher. The method offers high selectivity towards Cr(VI) against most competing metal ions. The calculated detection limit of this sensor was 5 nM.

Glutathione and diphenylcarbazide (DPC) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Merck Chemicals, India. Dialysis bags (MWCO: 500) was supplied by Sigma Aldrich, India. Double distilled water was used for all the studies.

SNCDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment glutathione in water at 220 °C for 3 h. Optimisation of the hydrothermal reaction conditions was carried out by monitoring the PL and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of an aqueous solution of glutathione at different temperatures (140, 180, 220 and 240 °C). TEM image of the SNCDs prepared at 220 °C reflects the formation of spherical particles, whereas the polymeric structures and partially formed dots were obtained at 140 and 180 °C, respectively. Further, the PL was broad and highest for 220 °C reaction, compared to sample prepared at 240 °C. Hence, glutathione treated at 220 °C was used for further studies. This colloidal solution of SNCDs was purified using a dialysis membrane of a molecular weight cut off 500 for 8 h. The pH of the resulting solution was ∼5. The pH of the purified solution was varied using dilute HCl or NaOH solution.

1.5 mL of purified SNCDs was mixed with 500 μL of 10−3 M DPC (dissolved in H2SO4) solution. This solution is considered as the sensor. To this, a previously prepared solution of K2Cr2O7 at various concentrations of analytes were introduced. The fluorescence spectra of all the solutions were recorded at 470 nm excitation. The emission and excitation slit widths were fixed at 3.5 nm.

UV–Visible spectra were collected using a Carry-100 UV–visible spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) studies were performed using a Spectrum 100T PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. For FTIR spectra, an aqueous solution of SNCDs was dried under vacuum at 50 °C and the resulting brownish semi-liquid obtained was mixed with KBr powder and the mixture was thoroughly ground, resulting in fine powder was converted into a pellet and the FTIR spectra were recorded using transmission mode. All steady state fluorescence measurements (excitation and emission) were carried out using FluoroMax-4C Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Instruments, USA). Both excitation and emission slit widths were fixed at 3.5 nm with an integration time of 0.1 ns. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed using time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC). The excitation wavelength was fixed at 344 nm for the TCSPC measurements with collection wavelengths of 390, 430 and 500 nm. Data analysis software (DAS) was used to fit the time resolved results and the best fit was accomplished by keeping minimal χ2 values in all the cases. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies of SNCDs were performed using an Omicron ESCA probe spectrometer with unmonochromatised Mg Kα radiation. EUTECH instruments’ pH meter was used for pH measurements.

The microscopy images (TEM and AFM) (Fig. 1 ) of purified SNCDs prepared at optimum hydrothermal reaction conditions demonstrate the formation of spherically sized particles (dots) with a size range of 10–20 nm (size histogram of ∼100 SNCDs in the TEM images is given as inset in Fig. 1). The thickness was ranging from 6 to 18 nm (AFM height profile is shown in Fig. 1 (B)), which is equivalent to 10–30 graphene layers [21,22].

FTIR spectra of SNCDs and glutathione (precursor) is presented in Fig. S1 (Supplementary material). For glutathione, peaks are at 3340 and 3245 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, 3122 and 3020 cm−1 represent the stretching band of N–H (NH3 +). The other peaks are ascribed as follows. 1710 cm−1: the stretching vibration of CO, 1592 & 1395 cm−1: the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of the carboxylate group, respectively, 1537 cm−1: the N–H deformation of the amide bond, 1592 cm−1: CC vibrations, and 1412 cm−1: C–N stretching of the amide group [23–25]. Whereas for SNCD, there is a shoulder peak at 1730 cm−1, which can be attributed to the –COOH group of SNCDs. The strong peak at 1654 cm−1 is assigned to the C=Nstr vibration and the sharp peak at 3087 cm−1 (absent in the case of glutathione) is attributed to the = C-Hstr vibrations present in SNCDs. A broad vibrational band at 3440 cm−1 refers to –OH/-NH groups of SNCDs. The successful incorporation of S into the carbon core of SNCDs can be assumed by the disappearance of S–H stretching feature at 2525 cm−1 [26].

The XPS survey scan spectrum of SNCDs shows the presence of C, O, N and S atoms as expected (Fig. S2). The two peaks at binding energy values of 100 and 152 eV in the survey scan spectrum correspond to the Si 2s and Si 2p, respectively, due to the SiO2 substrate [27]. The deconvoluted high-resolution C1s spectrum revealed the presence of CO (288.7 eV), C–N/C–O (286.2 eV), C–C/CN/C–H (285.0 eV) and CC (284.3 eV) groups (Fig. 2 (A) [28]. The successful incorporation of N into the carbon core was confirmed from the XPS peaks corresponds to graphitic, pyrrolic, pyridinic N atoms at 401.9, 400.5 and 399.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 (B)) [29]. The presence of –COOH and –OH functional groups at the surface of CDs was affirmed by the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2 (C)). The high-resolution S 2p peak was deconvoluted into four peaks correspond to S1/2 and S3/2 peaks of sulphide and thiophenic S, respectively (Fig. 2 (D)) [30]. Based on the above spectroscopic results, a representative structure of SNCDs, comprising of carbon core with pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic N atoms and S in the form of thiophinic and sulphide linkages decorated with –COOH, –OH and –NH2 functional groups at the surface was proposed (Fig. S3). UV–visible absorption spectrum of SNCDs shows a strong absorption band at ∼325 nm and two shoulder bands at ∼370 nm and ∼450 nm (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent PL response and understanding the origin of PL
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of SNCDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm show interesting results. The typical emission spectra of SNCDs encompassed three emission bands. A shoulder band at 390 nm (referred as ‘band I’) and a sharp emission at 430 nm (referred as ‘band II’) are excitation independent. An excitation-dependent emission feature was observed above 430 nm (referred as ‘band III’) at ≥370 nm excitation (Fig. 3 ). The same behaviour can be observed in the excitation-emission contour mapping of the SNCDs solution (Fig. S5). Many reported CDs are generally manifested either excitation dependent [31,32] or independent [33] emission properties. However, the present CDs is atypical, showing both excitation dependent and independent luminescence. Similar to the emission spectra, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of SNCDs obtained for 430 nm emission was comprised of three well recognisable excitation features (inset of Fig. 3), namely, a shoulder band at 245 nm and a strong excitation band at 325 nm and a strong and continuous excitation feature at 370 nm. Another noted observation was the pH-dependent luminescence response of band II and band III. This specifies the involvement of the energy levels corresponding to N, S and O containing functional groups at the surface and N and S present at the core of SNCDs. Whereas the pH-independent behaviour of the band I reflect the fact that the transition responsible for this band could be originated from SNCDs carbon core. To prove this hypothesis, we carried out detailed photophysical studies at different pH.

The PL intensities of the three emission bands (band I, band II and band III) mentioned above was evaluated at three different pH values viz. 2, 7 and 12 (given in Fig. 4 A–(C)). A plot of the intensity of band II (430 nm) with pH is shown in Fig. 4D. The effect of pH on band I (390 nm) and the excitation dependent emission at ∼460 nm (band III) is given in Fig. S6. These results show that the shoulder band observed at 390 nm (band I) in the emission spectrum of SNCDs has a negligible influence from the pH of the medium. However, the other two bands were influenced by the pH. Both intensity and position of the 430 nm emission band were affected by the pH.

The absorption, excitation and time-resolved fluorescence measurements were carried out to shed light on the observed steady-state PL response of SNCDs at different pH conditions. The UV–visible absorption features remain the same at all pH (Fig. S7). The excitation spectra recorded at different collection wavelengths show three excitation bands as in the case of absorption spectra. A high energy band centered ∼250 nm, a strong excitation peak ∼330 nm and a collection wavelength dependent low energy continuum band >360 nm (see Fig. 5 A–C). Like emission spectra, the position of the moderate energy excitation peak (330 nm) and low energy continuum (>360 nm) was highly influenced by the pH of the medium, whereas there was no substantial influence of pH on other excitation bands. A plot of excitation intensity at 330 nm v/s pH (Fig. 5 (D)) demonstrate the decrease of around 51% of PL intensity when the pH of the medium varied from 2 to 12.

It is reported that [16] the excitation feature at 245–250 nm (or absorption band below 300 nm) is due to the π−π* transition of the conjugated carbon core. The excitation at this energy probably led to the ∼390 nm emission (band I) must be originated from the carbon core. The pH of the medium did not influence both the intensity and position of the emission peak. This was supported by the fact that the carbon core is unaffected by the addition of H+ or OH− ions.

We assign the strong excitation feature at ∼330 nm (or absorption at 325 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectrum; Fig. S4) to the absorption responsible for the band II (430 nm) emission. This argument is supported by the fact that the emission intensity of band II (Fig. 4D) and its corresponding excitation peak intensity (Fig. 5D) was strongly affected by pH of the medium. It must be noted that emission position is not affected by the pH variation. The sharp excitation feature indicates fewer transitions responsible for this excitation band and is attributed to the excitation independent nature of band II. In general, the surface functional groups are expected to be affected strongly by the pH of the medium. Therefore, we cannot ascribe band II to the surface functional groups. For example, –COOH and –NH2 functional groups must be affected by the pH variation and reflected in emission position. Thus, we hypothesise that band II is due to the transition from non-bonding ‘n’ of the surface states (S and N atoms present at the core, not as the carboxyl or amine functional groups) to the π* orbitals of emissive traps (surface functional groups stabilised by CC). See Fig. S8 for a schematic representation of emissive states.

Finally, we assign the broad and a continuous excitation band to the emission band III. As already mentioned, band III showed excitation dependent nature. The broad and continuous excitation band indicates that it is composed of many closely located energy states. The optical selection of these energy levels must be responsible for the strong excitation dependency of band III. The N and S atoms (in the form of CN and CS) can create plenty of energy levels involving n, π and π* orbitals depending on the chemical environment. We attribute the excitation band observed at the low energy portion of the excitation spectrum could be due to the transition of electrons from ‘n’ orbitals of CN/CS to the corresponding π* orbitals (edge states). Since the energy gap between ‘n’ and π* orbitals of CN and CS is influenced by the nature of surface functional groups and the aromatic core surrounding these edges, many discrete sets of energy levels are possible. The excitation and subsequent radiative recombination might have led to excitation dependent band III. The possibility of protonation and deprotonation of these heteroatoms explains the variation of the PL intensity of band III with pH (∼56% decrease in PL intensity for a pH change of 2–12). Based on the above observations, an energy level diagram demonstrating the origin of PL emission of SNCDs at various excitation wavelengths has been proposed (Fig. 6 ). A representative structure of SNCDs with core, surface and edge states is given in Fig. S8.

The Stokes shifts in all the three bands are explained as follows. The substantial shift (>130 nm) in the band I can be attributed to the presence of differently sized carbon cores (as observed from TEM and AFM). The emission from the bigger core is assigned to the energy transfer/resonant re-absorption from smaller dots. The band II, manifested with a Stokes shift of ∼100 nm can be explained by the solvent-induced relaxation of the excited fluorophore. From a locally excited state (LE state), the fluorophore relaxes to the low energy solvent-induced relaxation state (SR state). Then subsequent recombination with ground state hole results in the emission at 430 nm. Because the LE state is controlled by the polar surface functional groups, which can interact strongly with surrounding water molecules before relaxing to the ground state. This solvent-induced relaxation is the most probable explanation for the observed significant Stokes shift in the case of band II. Finally, the direct recombination of excited state electrons from π* orbitals of the edge states to the corresponding ‘n’ orbitals without involving an intersystem crossing or energy transfer would be the likely reason for small Stokes shift values in the case of band III (Fig. 6).

With acidic to neutral pH, the decrease in PL could be due to the electron transfer from ‘n’ orbital of –NH2 to the ground state of excited fluorophore (Fig. S9). Such photoinduced electron transfer (PET) involving –NH2, –OH groups leading to the luminescence quenching of fluorophore is commonly seen in organic fluorophores [34]. Further, the decrease of luminescence in basic pH is explained as follows. At basic pH, most of the –COOH groups are deprotonated. Due to the high electron-withdrawing propensity of –COO−, a decrease in luminescence is envisaged. In general, electron-withdrawing groups are expected to destabilise the photo-excited state by removing the excited state electrons [35].

To support the observations, time-resolved emission spectra of SNCDs at multiple collection wavelengths has been measured (Table 1 and Fig. S10). The time-resolved PL decay of all collection wavelengths (390, 430 and 500 nm) at different pH are bi-exponential. The shorter component was ranging from 3.6 to 5.4 ns and the longer component was in 11.3–12.5 ns. The longer component can be attributed to the lifetime corresponding to band II emission, whereas the shorter component is assigned to the band I/band III. This argument is supported by the fact that the amplitude of the longer component was the highest at all pH conditions for the collection wavelength 430 nm. In contrast, the contribution from the shorter component was higher compared to that of the 430 nm collection wavelength for the other two collection wavelengths; 390 and 500 nm. When the collection wavelength was fixed at 430 nm, the amplitude of the longer component decreased continuously with the increase of pH. This further support the involvement of the surface group in band II.

We conducted FTIR investigations to understand the structural changes at different pH. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO− at 1590 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1, respectively) are absent in SNCDs at acidic pH, whereas a strong shoulder band is observed in basic and neutral pH. This observation indicates the deprotonation of carboxyl groups at basic pH. A peak at 1730 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the >CO group of carboxyl was found at acidic pH, whereas a weak band could be discerned at neutral and basic pH ranges. A strong vibration at 1260 cm−1 was observed for the C–O stretching in acidic pH. An additional peak at 1300 cm−1 was observed at acidic pH due to the C–O of the carboxyl group. The peak at 1668 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 are assigned to CN and CC groups, respectively, which are present at all pH. The peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 805 cm−1, assigned to the in-ring C–C aromatic stretch and = C–H out of the plane bend, respectively. The absence of S–H stretching vibration at 2525 cm−1 at all pH implicates the successful incorporation of S at the carbon core of SNCDs (Fig. S11).

The high fluorescence emission of SNCDs (photoluminescence quantum yield of 17.67%) along with their superior photostability (Fig. S12) were utilised their application in fluorescence-based sensing. Fig. 7 represents the fluorescence quenching based sensing strategy for Cr(VI) using SNCDs–diphenylcarbazide (DPC) dyad. DPC has used an analyte specific molecule. The detection was done using a solution of the SNCDs-DPC solution at pH 2. The emission of SNCDs was not affected by the DPC. During the detection step, quenching was observed in the presence of Cr(VI). The mechanism was attributed to the combined FRET and IFE, which are activated due to the interaction of Cr(VI) with DPC. Details on the mechanism of PL quenching by Cr(VI) will be discussed in a forthcoming section.

The decrease in the PL intensity of SNCDs-DPC mixture (the sensor solution) was proportional to the amount of the Cr(VI) added (Fig. 7 (A). A plot of the emission intensity v/s the concentration of Cr(VI) is a curve indicating the presence of multiple quenching mechanisms (Fig. 7 (B). The minimum concentration of the quencher at which a recognisable change in the PL emission of SNCDs, the limit of detection (LOD) was 5 nM. The specificity of SNCDs-DPC sensor solution was tested using various competing analytes such as metal ions and some commonly used organic molecules. As shown in Fig. S13, the response from other analytes was least compared to the response observed from Cr(VI), which demonstrates the effectiveness of the present system as Cr(VI) sensor. As a blank experiment, we have added the Cr(VI) solution to SNCDs solution alone (without DPC) and observed that there is no apparent effect on the fluorescent intensity (See Fig. S14).

UV–visible and time-resolved fluorescence analysis were carried out to understand the quenching mechanism. The absorption features of both SNCDs and DPC was not affected due to the presence of SNCDs (the pH was ∼2) (Fig. S15 (A)). This observation reflects that there was no interaction at electronic energy levels. On the contrary, strong absorption bands in the UV–visible spectrum have emerged in the presence of Cr(VI) (Fig. S15 (B)). These absorption bands have sufficient overlap with the emission of SNCDs. Such spectral overlap results in FRET-based quenching to operate and is the reason for the decrease in PL emission of SNCDs in the presence of Cr(VI). The spectral overlap between the newly emerged band and emission spectrum of SNCDs (excited at 470 nm) is shown in Fig. S15 (C). In addition to the spectral overlap, which is responsible for FRET, there exists a strong absorption background at 470 nm (the wavelength at which SNCDs was excited) (Fig. S15 (D)). This indicates that primary IFE may also be responsible for the quenching SNCDs’ emission apart from FRET. The results of time-resolved fluorescence spectra support our postulate. Both SNCDs and SNCDs-DPC solutions show a bi-exponential decay with the shorter component at ∼5 ns and a longer component at ∼9 ns (excitation: 451 nm, collection: 510 nm). But in the case of SNCDs-DPC-Cr(VI), a new ultrafast component with a lifetime of 0.32 ns have emerged. This component could be a non-radiative path, which arises due to the excited state energy transfer from donor to acceptor. This pathway is responsible for the quenching of SNCDs emission via FRET (Table S1).

The new absorption bands observed in the presence of Cr(VI) for SNCDs-DPC solution is due to the complexation of Cr(VI) with DPC and are well documented [36,37]. The strong absorption of this complex at the emission and excitation wavelength of SNCDs at acidic pH is responsible for the PL quenching of SNCDs and, in turn, operate during the selective detection of Cr (VI).

In conclusion, we synthesised SNCDs using glutathione as the precursor for both carbon and dopants through a hydrothermal route. The atypical PL emission behaviour of SNCDs, which shows both excitations dependent and independent PL response, was unravelled by the detailed photophysical studies at different pH. Fluorescence emission, excitation, lifetime and UV–visible absorption spectra at five different pH demonstrate the presence of multiple energy states in SNCDs viz. core, surface and edge states. A comprehensive energy level diagram, which reflects the origin of PL in SNCDs was proposed based on the results. Although further studies involving more advanced analytical tools may be required to completely understand the mechanism of photoluminescence in CDs and related luminophores, the work presented here could be a step towards this goal. The utilisation of SNCDs has been demonstrated for the selective quenching-based detection of Cr(VI). The PL quenching is surmised to arise from FRET as well as IFE. The limit of detection of the present sensor was found to be 5 nM with a dynamic range, 5 nM - 25 μM.

Manjunatha Ganiga (MG): Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Neema Pallikkarathodi Mani (NPM): Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jobin Cyriac (JC): Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge IIST Thiruvananthapuram for funding, PSG Institute of Advanced Studies- Coimbatore and National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram for analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.118767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: large-scale synthesis, sensing and bioimaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wang, K.-Q. Lu, Z.-R. Tang, and Y.-J. Xu, "Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis," Journal of Materials Chemistry A, vol. 5, pp. 3717-3734, 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid mediated organophilic carbon dots for drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shu, J. Lu, Q.-X. Mao, R.-S. Song, X.-Y. Wang, X.-W. Chen, and J.-H. Wang, "Ionic liquid mediated organophilic carbon dots for drug delivery and bioimaging," Carbon, vol. 114, pp. 324-333, 2017.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.-J. Wang, X. Hai, Q.-X. Mao, M.-L. Chen, and J.-H. Wang, "Polyhedral Oligomeric Silsesquioxane Functionalized Carbon Dots for Cell Imaging," ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, vol. 7, pp. 16609-16616, 2015/08/05 2015.
F. Wang, Z. Xie, H. Zhang, C.-y. Liu, and Y.-g. Zhang, "Highly Luminescent Organosilane-Functionalised Carbon Dots," Advanced Functional Materials, vol. 21, pp. 1027-1031, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue fluorescent carbon thin films fabricated from dodecylamine-capped carbon nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Pan, J. Zhang, Z. Li, Z. Zhang, L. Guo, and M. Wu, "Blue fluorescent carbon thin films fabricated from dodecylamine-capped carbon nanoparticles," Journal of Materials Chemistry, vol. 21, pp. 3565-3567, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular origin of photoluminescence of carbon dots: aggregation-induced orange-red emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Gude, A. Das, T. Chatterjee, and P. K. Mandal, "Molecular origin of photoluminescence of carbon dots: aggregation-induced orange-red emission," Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics, vol. 18, pp. 28274-28280, 2016.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Lu, R. Cong, S. Zhu, X. Zhao, J. Liu, J. S. Tse, S. Meng, and B. Yang, "pH-Dependent Synthesis of Novel Structure-Controllable Polymer-Carbon NanoDots with High Acidophilic Luminescence and Super Carbon Dots Assembly for White Light-Emitting Diodes," ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, vol. 8, pp. 4062-4068, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence of carbon nanodots: dipole emission centers and electron–phonon coupling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, A. M. Chizhik, N. Karedla, M. O. Dekaliuk, I. Gregor, H. Schuhmann, M. Seibt, K. Bodensiek, I. A. T. Schaap, O. Schulz, A. P. Demchenko, J. Enderlein, and A. I. Chizhik, "Photoluminescence of Carbon Nanodots: Dipole Emission Centers and Electron-Phonon Coupling," Nano Letters, vol. 14, pp. 5656-5661, 2014/10/08 2014.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.-B. Yu, J.-S. Wei, and H.-M. Xiong, "Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism," Acs Nano, vol. 10, pp. 484-491, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a unique fluorescent cocktail of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Dhenadhayalan, K.-C. Lin, R. Suresh, and P. Ramamurthy, "Unravelling the multiple emissive states in citric-acid-derived carbon dots," The Journal of Physical Chemistry C, vol. 120, pp. 1252-1261, 2016.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, J. Pang, F. Xu, and X. Zhang, "Simple approach to synthesise amino-functionalised carbon dots by carbonisation of chitosan," Scientific reports, vol. 6, p. 31100, 2016.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Padarauskas, A. Judzentien, E. Naujalis, and V. Paliulionyt, "On-line preconcentration and determination of chromium (VI) in waters by high-performance liquid chromatography using pre-column complexation with 1, 5-diphenylcarbazide," Journal of Chromatography A, vol. 808, pp. 193-199, 1998.
